parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Enumerate inv_id values, 2) find one owned by another user, 3) PATCH note field, 4) confirm 200 response with modified data.

parsed_findings.proof: db.get_invoice(inv_id) fetches any invoice by attacker-supplied ID; if inv has no project_id (e.g. deleted/null), user_can_edit may return truthy or raise, bypassing the 403.

parsed_findings.vuln_claim: IDOR via unvalidated inv_id before ownership check

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) PATCH with note=<script>alert(1)</script>, 2) retrieve invoice via GET, 3) confirm payload stored and reflected.

parsed_findings.proof: request.json.get('note', inv.note) accepts arbitrary attacker-controlled string with no sanitization, length cap, or type check before persistence.

parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No input validation or length/type constraint on note field allows storing malicious or oversized content.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Mass assignment -> privilege escalation -> full account takeover or admin access
parsed_findings.impact: Full privilege escalation; any user can promote themselves to admin or overwrite sensitive fields.
parsed_findings.proof: PATCH /api/users/123 with body {"isAdmin":true} merges directly into the user object before save.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send PATCH /api/users/:id with {"isAdmin":true} as an authenticated user; check DB for privilege escalation.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Mass assignment via Object.assign on req.body allows overwriting arbitrary user fields (e.g. isAdmin, role, password).

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Broken authz -> bypass guard -> combine with mass assignment for full privilege escalation
parsed_findings.impact: Broken access control; any user may bypass the intended owner/support guard and modify any user record.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 reads `req.user.id req.user.isSupport` which is a syntax/logic error — likely meant `!==` or `&&`; parsed as expression statement, always falsy or throws.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send any PATCH request as an unauthenticated or low-priv user; if condition short-circuits, access proceeds without proper authz check.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authorization check uses a likely broken condition (missing logical operator between req.user.id and req.user.isSupport) allowing unintended access.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: IDOR -> arbitrary user record write -> chain with mass assignment for vertical escalation
parsed_findings.impact: Horizontal privilege escalation; full data modification of any user account in the system.
parsed_findings.proof: req.params.id is never compared to req.user.id after the broken guard; attacker sets id to any victim's ID.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticated as user A, PATCH /api/users/<user_B_id> with arbitrary body; record B is updated with no further check.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No ownership check after findById means any authenticated user can modify any other user's record (IDOR).

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit race -> drain victim balance -> chain to repeated calls for amplified loss
parsed_findings.impact: Full balance theft; attacker can drain account to negative, effectively creating funds from nothing.
parsed_findings.proof: Two concurrent POST /transfer requests both read src balance before either write completes, passing the src<amt check twice and draining more than available.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two simultaneous requests with amount=full_balance; both threads read same src, both pass check, both deduct, resulting in negative balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Race condition (TOCTOU) allows double-spend via concurrent transfer requests

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send negative amount -> sender balance increases -> receiver credited -> chain with race condition for maximum impact
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker credits own account and inflates sender balance simultaneously with a single request.
parsed_findings.proof: int(request.json['amount']) with no positivity check; sending amount=-100 passes src<amt (e.g. 0 < -100 is False only if src>=0, but negative amt makes src-amt > src).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with amount=-100 and to=attacker_id; src - (-100) increases sender balance while crediting attacker.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No input validation on 'to' or 'amount' fields allows negative amount transfer to credit attacker

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send to invalid ID -> funds deducted from src -> potential silent loss or ghost account creation
parsed_findings.impact: Funds destruction or unauthorized account creation depending on DB semantics; enables griefing or accounting discrepancies.
parsed_findings.proof: db.get_balance(to) is called without verifying 'to' exists; funds may be sent into a void or to unintended accounts depending on DB behavior.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with to=nonexistent_id; if db returns 0 for unknown user, funds leave src but are credited nowhere, effectively burning them or creating ghost accounts.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No authorization check that 'to' is a valid existing user allows transfers to arbitrary/nonexistent IDs

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge JWT with arbitrary claims → trigger verify failure (wrong key/alg) → production catch returns decoded payload → downstream code trusts claims as authenticated
parsed_findings.impact: Complete authentication bypass in production; attacker can forge arbitrary claims (sub, role, etc.) and impersonate any user.
parsed_findings.proof: If jwt.verify throws (e.g. expired, wrong key, or attacker-supplied 'none' alg), production env falls through to jwt.decode which performs NO signature verification.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with a JWT signed by an arbitrary key or with alg:none; in production the catch block calls jwt.decode and returns the unverified payload as trusted identity.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: JWT signature verification silently bypassed in production on any verify failure

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit timing leak to recover SECRET, then forge valid X-Sig for arbitrary body and call process_event with attacker-controlled JSON.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can brute-force the HMAC signature and forge arbitrary webhook payloads to trigger process_event.
parsed_findings.proof: got == want uses Python str equality which short-circuits on first differing byte, leaking timing info about the correct signature.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests with signatures differing at each byte position and measure response latency to recover SECRET byte-by-byte.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Timing side-channel in HMAC comparison via non-constant-time == operator

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Chain after CWE-208 to forge signature, then deliver malicious JSON payload to process_event.
parsed_findings.impact: Depending on process_event internals, malformed JSON structure could trigger DoS, injection, or logic-bypass in downstream processing.
parsed_findings.proof: json.loads(body) is called inside the signature-valid branch with no schema or type validation before handing to process_event.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: After obtaining valid signature (via timing attack), send a crafted JSON body with unexpected keys/types to probe process_event for injection or logic bugs.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unsanitized json.loads of raw request body passed directly to process_event without validation

parsed_findings.chain_steps: null out bypasses guard -> memcpy(NULL,in,n) -> null-ptr write -> crash/controlled write if page 0 mapped
parsed_findings.impact: Null pointer write leading to crash (DoS) or, on targets with mapped page 0, potential code execution.
parsed_findings.proof: if (out || out_sz == 0) — non-null out is truthy, returning -1 on valid input; null out is falsy, proceeding to memcpy(NULL,...).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call copy_name("hello", NULL, 10): out=NULL is falsy so guard passes, strnlen and memcpy execute with out=NULL causing null-ptr deref/write.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Guard condition uses `||` instead of `!out ||`, so null `out` passes the check and memcpy writes through a null pointer.
